title: Untitled
research_problem: Identify factors affecting pedestrian red light running violations at signalized urban intersections, including personal factors (demographics, behavior) and environmental factors (signal time, traffic conditions, social influences)
method_summary: Video observation surveys conducted at six signalized crosswalks in Hong Kong to capture crossing behaviors of 6320 pedestrians. Random parameter logit model developed to measure association between red light running propensity and explanatory factors, accounting for unobserved heterogeneity.
datasets: 6320 pedestrians observed at 6 signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong
652 signal cycles captured during weekday peak and non-peak periods
Sites selected as pedestrian crash hot spots (each with >10 pedestrian crashes in preceding 5 years)

main_claims.claim_text: Female pedestrians have significantly lower propensity to run red lights compared to males (odds ratio = 0.667)
Elderly pedestrians have higher red light running propensity than adults, contrary to some previous findings
Presence of a companion significantly reduces red light running propensity (odds ratio = 0.579), and presence of other violators increases it (odds ratio = 4.023)
Traffic volume and percentage of heavy vehicles are negatively associated with red light running propensity
Significant interaction effects exist between gender and traffic volume, gender and other violators, elderly and companion status on red light running propensity

limitations.text: Study focuses on propensity but does not measure safety outcomes (crash risk) associated with red light running behavior
Effect of vehicular speed on pedestrian red light running behavior is not assessed
Findings may be specific to urban Hong Kong context with no pedestrian countdown displays; generalizability to other settings may be limited
Data on pedestrian safety perception, attitude, and detailed socioeconomic characteristics not available in observation survey

future_work: Explore effectiveness of pedestrian signal countdown devices in reducing violations
Investigate interactions between vehicular speed and pedestrian characteristics
Develop attitudinal models to gauge safety perception effects
Evaluate variable pedestrian signal time responsive to pedestrian volume
Examine effects of social norms and anticipated traffic conditions using comprehensive surveys
notes: Random parameter approach used to account for unobserved heterogeneity in pedestrian crossing decisions; Both individual-level (pedestrian demographics) and cycle-level (traffic condition, signal time) factors incorporated; Study addresses gap in literature by examining combined effects of personal and environmental factors with interaction terms; Model 2 with interaction effects showed superior fit (lower AIC = 2750.1 vs 2776.6); 18.5% of observed pedestrians (1169/6320) committed red light running violations
